enrichment.short_description: AI-first customer service platform pairing the Fin AI Agent with a shared inbox, live chat, and help center tools.
enrichment.seo_title: Intercom Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Intercom review covering the Fin AI Agent, pricing tiers, features, pros and cons, and how it compares to Zendesk and Freshdesk for support teams.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Intercom is a customer service and engagement platform founded in 2011 in San Francisco by four Irish entrepreneurs, Eoghan McCabe, Des Traynor, Ciaran Lee and David Barrett, who built it as a tool to help SaaS companies talk to customers directly inside their own products; the company grew out of Dublin's tech scene before basing its headquarters in San Francisco, and it also maintains offices in Chicago, Dublin, Sydney and London. Over its history Intercom raised roughly 490 million dollars in venture funding across a seed round in 2012, a 6 million dollar Series A in 2013 led by Social Capital, a 23 million dollar Series B in 2014 led by Bessemer Venture Partners, a Series C round led by Index Ventures, and a 125 million dollar Series D in 2018 led by Kleiner Perkins with participation from Google Ventures; in 2026 the company also took on 250 million dollars in venture debt from Hercules Capital, and in June 2026 Salesforce announced a definitive agreement to acquire Intercom for approximately 3.6 billion dollars, with the product rebranded around its AI agent, Fin. Architecturally, Intercom is a cloud-based SaaS platform built around a shared team inbox, a customizable Messenger widget embedded in web and mobile apps, and a knowledge base/help center, all unified by Fin, an AI customer service agent introduced in 2023 that reads a company's help center and support history to autonomously resolve customer questions across chat, email and voice, escalating to human agents when needed. Core features include the shared inbox with macros and canned responses, live chat and in-app messaging, a public and private help center, proactive support tools such as banners, tours and outbound campaigns, a no-code workflow/automation builder for routing and triage, Fin Copilot which gives human agents AI-drafted replies, multilingual and multibrand support, SLA management, CSAT surveys, and prebuilt plus custom analytics dashboards; integrations cover Salesforce, HubSpot, Slack, Jira, Shopify and dozens of other tools through an open API. Pricing is structured around three seat-based plans billed per agent: Essential starts at 29 dollars per seat per month billed annually (39 dollars billed monthly) and includes the shared inbox, live chat, basic automation and a public help center; Advanced runs roughly 85 to 99 dollars per seat per month and adds multiple inboxes, an automation builder, round-robin assignment and a private or multilingual help center; Expert runs roughly 132 to 139 dollars per seat per month and adds SSO, HIPAA-eligible configurations, SLAs and multibrand messengers. Fin is billed separately on a resolution-based model at 0.99 dollars per successful outcome regardless of how many messages that resolution took, and it can also be deployed standalone on top of an existing helpdesk like Salesforce Service Cloud without requiring seat licenses; add-ons such as Fin Copilot, a Proactive Support Plus package, and pay-as-you-go channels like SMS and WhatsApp add further cost, so real-world spend for AI-heavy teams is often several times the sticker price. Intercom targets mid-market and enterprise SaaS, e-commerce and tech companies that need to combine live human support with AI automation, and it competes most directly with Zendesk, Freshdesk, HubSpot Service Hub, Salesforce Service Cloud and AI-native entrants like Ada and Decagon; its differentiation has historically been its unified messaging-first product design and, more recently, Fin's resolution-based pricing and deep integration with its own inbox and help center rather than being bolted onto a legacy ticketing system.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Intercom is a customer service platform that combines a shared team inbox, live chat Messenger, help center and AI automation into one product. Founded in 2011 by Eoghan McCabe, Des Traynor, Ciaran Lee and David Barrett, it was one of the earliest companies to popularize in-app messaging as an alternative to email-only support, and it has since expanded into a full support suite used by thousands of SaaS, e-commerce and technology companies.

The centerpiece of Intercom's current product is Fin, an AI customer service agent launched in 2023 that reads a company's help center and past conversations to resolve customer questions autonomously across chat, email and voice, handing off to human agents only when needed. In June 2026, Salesforce agreed to acquire Intercom for approximately 3.6 billion dollars, folding the Fin AI agent and support suite into Salesforce's broader customer platform while Intercom continues to operate its existing product line.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: At its core, Intercom offers a shared inbox with macros, canned responses and team assignment rules, a customizable Messenger widget for live chat and in-app messaging, and a public or private help center for self-service articles. A no-code workflow automation builder handles routing, triage and proactive outbound campaigns like banners and product tours, while reporting dashboards track response times, resolution rates and customer satisfaction.

Fin AI Agent and Fin Copilot are the platform's AI layer: Fin resolves customer questions directly, while Copilot assists human agents with AI-drafted replies and internal knowledge lookups. Higher tiers add SLA management, multibrand and multilingual help centers, SSO/identity management, and HIPAA-eligible configurations for regulated industries, alongside native integrations with Salesforce, HubSpot, Slack, Jira and Shopify.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Intercom prices its core plans per seat: Essential starts at 29 dollars per seat per month (annual billing), Advanced runs roughly 85 to 99 dollars per seat per month, and Expert runs roughly 132 to 139 dollars per seat per month, with each tier unlocking more automation, routing, and enterprise controls. All plans include the Fin AI Agent, but Fin itself is billed separately at 0.99 dollars per resolved outcome rather than being bundled into the seat price.

Because of this combination of seat costs, per-outcome AI charges and optional add-ons like Fin Copilot, Proactive Support Plus, and pay-as-you-go SMS/WhatsApp channels, real-world Intercom bills for AI-heavy support teams frequently run well above the advertised starting price. Fin can also be purchased as a standalone AI agent layered on top of an existing helpdesk such as Salesforce Service Cloud, with no seat costs required.

features.title: Fin AI Agent
features.description: Autonomous AI agent that resolves customer questions across chat, email and voice using the company's help center and support history.

features.title: Shared Team Inbox
features.description: Unified inbox with macros, canned responses and assignment rules for collaborative ticket handling.

features.title: Messenger and Live Chat
features.description: Customizable in-app and web chat widget for real-time customer conversations.

features.title: Help Center
features.description: Public or private self-service knowledge base with multilingual and multibrand support at higher tiers.

features.title: Workflow Automation Builder
features.description: No-code tool for building routing rules, triage flows and proactive outbound campaigns.

features.title: Fin Copilot
features.description: AI assistant that drafts replies and surfaces internal knowledge for human support agents.

features.title: SLA and CSAT Management
features.description: Service-level tracking, escalation rules and customer satisfaction surveys on higher-tier plans.

features.title: Reporting and Analytics
features.description: Prebuilt and custom dashboards for response time, resolution rate and team performance.

pros: Fin AI Agent resolves a large share of routine tickets without human involvement, cutting response times
Unified messenger, inbox and help center reduce the need for multiple point tools
Deep proactive messaging tools like banners, tours and outbound campaigns go beyond typical helpdesk software
Strong native integrations with Salesforce, HubSpot, Slack and other business tools
Frequent feature releases backed by heavy ongoing investment, now under Salesforce
cons: Pricing is complex and can escalate quickly once Fin AI outcomes, seats and add-ons are combined
Advanced features like the workflow builder and SSO are locked behind higher-priced tiers
Some users report a steep learning curve for setting up automations and reporting
Per-outcome Fin pricing makes cost forecasting difficult for high-volume support teams
Historically positioned at a premium price point compared to Zendesk or Freshdesk for small teams

faqs.question: What is Intercom used for?
faqs.answer: Intercom is a customer service platform combining a shared inbox, live chat, a help center and an AI agent (Fin) to handle and automate customer support conversations.

faqs.question: How much does Intercom cost?
faqs.answer: Core plans start at 29 dollars per seat per month billed annually, with Advanced and Expert tiers running higher; the Fin AI Agent is billed separately at 0.99 dollars per resolved outcome.

faqs.question: What is Fin AI Agent?
faqs.answer: Fin is Intercom's AI customer service agent that autonomously answers customer questions using a company's help center and support history, escalating to humans when it cannot resolve an issue.

faqs.question: Is there a free plan for Intercom?
faqs.answer: Intercom does not offer a permanent free plan, but it does offer a free trial so teams can test the platform before subscribing.

faqs.question: Who owns Intercom now?
faqs.answer: In June 2026, Salesforce announced a definitive agreement to acquire Intercom for approximately 3.6 billion dollars.

faqs.question: What are the main Intercom alternatives?
faqs.answer: Common alternatives include Zendesk, Freshdesk, HubSpot Service Hub and Salesforce Service Cloud.

features: Works with existing helpdesks like Salesforce
No seat costs or setup fees

enrichment.short_description: Zero-knowledge encrypted cloud storage from Spain with post-quantum encryption for files, sharing, and email.
enrichment.seo_title: Internxt Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Internxt review covering zero-knowledge encryption, pricing and lifetime plans, features, pros and cons versus Tresorit, pCloud and Google Drive.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Internxt is a privacy-focused cloud storage company founded in 2020 by Spanish entrepreneur Fran Villalba Segarra and headquartered in Valencia, Spain, built around the premise that cloud providers should never be able to read a customer's files. The company has raised roughly 8.46 million dollars in funding across multiple rounds, including early crowdfunding and equity rounds, positioning itself as a European, GDPR-native alternative to US-based providers like Google Drive and Dropbox, and markets its mission as building a more decentralized, user-owned 'internet of the future.' Technically, Internxt is architected around zero-knowledge, client-side AES-256 encryption combined with post-quantum cryptography, meaning files are encrypted on the user's device before upload and Internxt itself holds no keys capable of decrypting stored data; the company also shards and distributes encrypted file blocks across its infrastructure rather than storing whole files on a single server. The core product, Internxt Drive, is complemented by a growing privacy suite that includes Internxt Send for encrypted file transfer, Internxt Meet for encrypted video calls, Internxt Mail, a VPN, an antivirus and malware scanner, and a storage cleaner utility, all accessible via native apps for Windows, macOS, Linux, iOS and Android as well as a web client, CLI and WebDAV support; much of the client-side code is open source and published on GitHub, and the company has undergone independent penetration testing by Securitum plus holds GDPR, HIPAA, ISO 27001 and SOC 2 compliance credentials. Pricing follows a freemium model: every account gets 1GB of storage free for life with no credit card required; above that, Internxt offers subscription plans across Essential (1TB), Premium (3TB) and Ultimate (5TB) tiers, billed monthly or annually with a discount for annual billing, plus one-time lifetime plans ranging from roughly 450 to 950 dollars for storage up to 50TB that never expire and avoid recurring fees, a pricing structure that is unusual among privacy cloud providers and appeals to users wary of subscription creep. All paid tiers bundle VPN access, antivirus scanning, password-protected link sharing and file versioning rather than gating those features behind separate add-ons. The target audience is privacy-conscious individuals, journalists, EU businesses subject to GDPR, and technical users who want an open-source, self-verifiable alternative to mainstream cloud storage; Internxt's most direct competitors are other zero-knowledge providers such as Tresorit, pCloud, Proton Drive and Sync.com, against which Internxt differentiates primarily on its post-quantum encryption claim, its lifetime-plan pricing option, and its largely open-source client codebase, while trailing larger incumbents like Google Drive and Dropbox in ecosystem integrations and brand recognition.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Internxt is a Valencia, Spain-based cloud storage company founded in 2020 by Fran Villalba Segarra with a single design premise: the provider itself should never be able to read a customer's files. It positions itself as a European, privacy-first alternative to Google Drive and Dropbox, built on zero-knowledge, client-side encryption rather than server-side encryption that the vendor can unlock.

Beyond core file storage, Internxt has expanded into a broader privacy suite including encrypted file transfer, video calling, email, a VPN and an antivirus tool, reflecting an ambition to offer a full alternative to mainstream cloud and productivity services for privacy-conscious users and GDPR-bound organizations.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Internxt Drive encrypts files with AES-256 client-side encryption before upload and layers in post-quantum cryptography, meaning no one but the account holder, not even Internxt staff, can decrypt stored data. Encrypted files are also sharded and distributed across infrastructure rather than kept as single whole files, adding a further layer of protection against any single point of compromise.

The platform ships native apps for Windows, macOS, Linux, iOS and Android plus a web client, CLI and WebDAV support, and bundles password-protected file sharing, file versioning, and the wider Internxt suite (Send, Meet, Mail, VPN, antivirus and a storage cleaner) into paid plans. Much of the client code is open source on GitHub, and the company has been independently penetration-tested and holds GDPR, HIPAA, ISO 27001 and SOC 2 compliance credentials.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Every Internxt account starts with 1GB of storage free for life with no credit card required. Paid subscription tiers scale from Essential (1TB) through Premium (3TB) to Ultimate (5TB), billed monthly or at a discount annually, with all tiers including the VPN, antivirus and password-protected sharing features rather than gating them behind separate add-ons.

Internxt also offers one-time lifetime plans, ranging from roughly 450 to 950 dollars for one-time storage allotments up to 50TB that never expire and carry no recurring fee, a pricing model uncommon among privacy-focused cloud providers and one the company uses to differentiate itself from subscription-only competitors like Tresorit and pCloud.

features.title: Zero-knowledge encryption
features.description: Client-side AES-256 encryption ensures only the account holder can decrypt stored files.

features.title: Post-quantum cryptography
features.description: Additional encryption layer designed to resist future quantum-computing-based attacks.

features.title: Sharded file storage
features.description: Encrypted files are split and distributed across infrastructure rather than stored as single files.

features.title: Internxt Send
features.description: Encrypted file transfer tool for sharing large files securely with external recipients.

features.title: Internxt Meet and Mail
features.description: Encrypted video calling and email products extending the privacy suite beyond storage.

features.title: Cross-platform apps
features.description: Native clients for Windows, macOS, Linux, iOS and Android plus web, CLI and WebDAV access.

features.title: Lifetime storage plans
features.description: One-time payment plans for permanent storage allocations without recurring subscription fees.

features.title: Compliance certifications
features.description: GDPR, HIPAA, ISO 27001 and SOC 2 compliance backed by independent penetration testing.

pros: True zero-knowledge encryption means Internxt cannot access or hand over readable user data
One-time lifetime storage plans avoid ongoing subscription costs
Post-quantum encryption is a forward-looking differentiator versus most competitors
EU-based (Spain) hosting appeals to GDPR-conscious individuals and businesses
Bundled VPN, antivirus and encrypted sharing add value beyond plain storage
cons: Smaller company and ecosystem than incumbents like Google Drive or Dropbox
Fewer third-party app integrations compared to mainstream cloud storage providers
Free tier storage (1GB) is modest compared to some competitors' free offers
Collaboration features (real-time document editing, etc.) are less developed than Google Workspace
Zero-knowledge design means lost passwords can result in permanently inaccessible data

faqs.question: What is Internxt used for?
faqs.answer: Internxt is an encrypted cloud storage service used to securely store, sync and share files, photos and documents with zero-knowledge, client-side encryption.

faqs.question: Is Internxt really zero-knowledge?
faqs.answer: Yes, files are encrypted on the user's device before upload using AES-256 and post-quantum cryptography, so Internxt itself cannot read stored data.

faqs.question: Does Internxt have a free plan?
faqs.answer: Yes, every account includes 1GB of free storage for life with no credit card required.

faqs.question: What are Internxt lifetime plans?
faqs.answer: Lifetime plans are one-time payments, roughly 450 to 950 dollars, for storage allocations up to 50TB that never expire.

faqs.question: Where is Internxt based?
faqs.answer: Internxt is headquartered in Valencia, Spain, and operates under EU data protection law.

faqs.question: What are Internxt alternatives?
faqs.answer: Common alternatives include Tresorit, pCloud, Proton Drive, Sync.com, Google Drive and Dropbox.

use_cases.title: Privacy-conscious personal storage
use_cases.description: Individuals store photos, documents and backups with encryption the provider itself cannot read.

use_cases.title: GDPR-compliant business storage
use_cases.description: EU businesses use Internxt for file storage that satisfies GDPR data residency and protection requirements.

use_cases.title: Long-term archival with lifetime plans
use_cases.description: Users who want to avoid recurring subscriptions pay once for a fixed storage allocation.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source, turn-key research data repository platform built on CERN's Invenio framework, powering Zenodo.
enrichment.seo_title: InvenioRDM Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: InvenioRDM review covering the open-source CERN repository platform behind Zenodo, its architecture, features, and comparison to DSpace and Dataverse.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: InvenioRDM is a free, open-source research data management repository platform developed by CERN and a distributed community of research institutions, built on top of CERN's long-running Invenio framework, which the organization originally created to power large-scale document repositories such as the CERN Document Server and, later, the preprint and dataset repository Zenodo, launched in 2013 in partnership with OpenAIRE and the European Commission. InvenioRDM was conceived as a next-generation, turn-key rebuild of that framework aimed at giving any research institution the same repository capabilities that power Zenodo, without requiring institutions to build custom software; the project reached its v1.0 milestone in February 2021 as an early demo-ready release, followed by the first production-suitable release, v6.0, in August 2021, and Zenodo itself completed its migration onto the InvenioRDM codebase in October 2023, validating the platform at very large scale across millions of records and petabytes of data. Governance is led by CERN for the benefit of the wider community, with active development and steering contributions from partner institutions including Caltech, TU Graz, Northwestern University and CERN openlab under the umbrella of the Invenio Collaboration; the project is fully open source, with all code hosted publicly on GitHub. Architecturally, InvenioRDM is a Python-based application built on Flask and Invenio-Records-Resources, using PostgreSQL for relational data, OpenSearch or Elasticsearch for full-text and faceted search, Celery and Redis for background task processing, and a React and SASS-based frontend; it exposes REST and OAI-PMH APIs, integrates with DataCite for DOI minting, supports ORCID and ROR identifiers, controlled vocabularies, community-based curation workflows, granular access controls and embargoes, versioned records, and rich metadata schemas aligned with FAIR data principles, and it is designed to scale from a handful of records to well over a hundred million, deployable on bare metal, virtual machines, or container platforms like Docker and Kubernetes. Because it is open-source software rather than a commercial SaaS product, there is no license fee: institutions download and self-host InvenioRDM for free, though they bear their own infrastructure and systems-administration costs, and a small number of managed-hosting vendors and consultancies offer paid implementation and hosting services on top of the free codebase. The target audience is universities, national laboratories, scientific consortia, libraries and funders that need to host FAIR, citable, DOI-assigned research datasets, software and publications in compliance with funder open-data mandates such as those from the EU, NIH and NSF; InvenioRDM's most direct competitors are other open-source repository platforms such as DSpace, Dataverse, Fedora and CKAN, as well as commercial hosted options like Figshare, and it differentiates itself through its direct lineage to Zenodo, arguably the most widely used general-purpose research data repository in the world, its modern API-first architecture, and its backing by CERN's decades of large-scale scientific data infrastructure experience.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: InvenioRDM is a free, open-source repository platform for managing and publishing research data, software and publications, built by CERN and a global community of research institutions on top of CERN's Invenio framework. It was designed as a turn-key rebuild that gives any institution the same repository capabilities that power Zenodo, the widely used European open-access research repository.

InvenioRDM's first demo-ready release, v1.0, arrived in February 2021, with the first production-suitable release (v6.0) following in August 2021. In October 2023, Zenodo itself completed its migration onto the InvenioRDM codebase, proving the platform can operate at massive scale across millions of records and petabytes of data.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The platform is built on a Python and Flask backend with PostgreSQL, OpenSearch/Elasticsearch and Celery/Redis, exposing REST and OAI-PMH APIs and a React-based frontend. It integrates with DataCite for DOI minting and supports ORCID and ROR persistent identifiers, controlled vocabularies, and metadata schemas aligned with FAIR data principles.

InvenioRDM supports community-based curation workflows, granular access controls and embargoes, and full record versioning, and it is designed to scale from small departmental archives to repositories with well over a hundred million records, deployable via Docker, Kubernetes, or traditional server infrastructure.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: InvenioRDM is free, open-source software with no license fee. Institutions download the codebase and self-host it on their own infrastructure, meaning the only ongoing cost is hosting, systems administration and any internal staff time devoted to customization and maintenance.

For institutions without in-house DevOps capacity, a small number of specialist consultancies and hosting partners affiliated with the Invenio community offer paid implementation and managed-hosting services layered on top of the free core software, providing an alternative to fully self-managed deployments.

features.title: Turn-key repository deployment
features.description: Pre-built, configurable repository platform ready to deploy without building custom software from scratch.

features.title: DOI minting via DataCite
features.description: Automatic assignment of citable, persistent digital object identifiers to published records.

features.title: FAIR metadata and controlled vocabularies
features.description: Rich, standards-aligned metadata schemas supporting findable, accessible, interoperable and reusable data.

features.title: Community curation workflows
features.description: Configurable review and curation processes for institutional or subject-based communities.

features.title: Versioned records and embargoes
features.description: Full version history alongside granular access controls and time-limited embargoes on records.

features.title: REST and OAI-PMH APIs
features.description: Open APIs for programmatic ingestion, harvesting and integration with other research systems.

features.title: Scalable architecture
features.description: Container-friendly design (Docker, Kubernetes) proven at Zenodo scale, from single records to over a hundred million.

features.title: ORCID and ROR integration
features.description: Persistent identifier support for linking authors and institutions to published research outputs.

pros: Free and open source with no licensing costs
Proven at massive scale via Zenodo, which runs on the same codebase
Backed by CERN and an active multi-institution developer community
FAIR-compliant metadata and DOI support built in from the start
Modern, API-first architecture that integrates well with other research systems
cons: Requires technical expertise in Python, Docker and Kubernetes to self-host and maintain
No official vendor support line; institutions rely on community forums or paid consultancies
Initial setup and metadata customization can be time-consuming for smaller institutions
Fewer out-of-the-box integrations than commercial platforms like Figshare
Ongoing maintenance is required to track releases and community-driven changes

faqs.question: What is InvenioRDM used for?
faqs.answer: InvenioRDM is used by universities, labs and consortia to host, curate and publish FAIR, citable research datasets, software and publications.

faqs.question: Is InvenioRDM free?
faqs.answer: Yes, it is free, open-source software; institutions bear only their own hosting and administration costs.

faqs.question: What is the relationship between InvenioRDM and Zenodo?
faqs.answer: Zenodo, the well-known European open-access repository, migrated onto the InvenioRDM codebase in October 2023, making it the platform's largest real-world deployment.

faqs.question: Who maintains InvenioRDM?
faqs.answer: CERN leads governance of the project for the benefit of the community, with contributions from institutions including Caltech, TU Graz and Northwestern University.

faqs.question: Does InvenioRDM assign DOIs?
faqs.answer: Yes, it integrates with DataCite to automatically mint persistent DOIs for published records.

faqs.question: What are alternatives to InvenioRDM?
faqs.answer: Common alternatives include DSpace, Dataverse, Fedora, CKAN and the commercial platform Figshare.

use_cases.title: University research data repository
use_cases.description: Universities host and publish FAIR datasets, code and publications produced by their researchers.

use_cases.title: National lab and consortium data archiving
use_cases.description: Scientific consortia archive large-scale experimental and observational datasets with DOI-based citation.

use_cases.title: Institutional open-access publication repository
use_cases.description: Institutions comply with funder open-data mandates by publishing citable, versioned outputs.

features: Community support via forums and GitHub

enrichment.short_description: Free, open-source peer-to-peer app for cataloguing personal book collections and lending, sharing or gifting books.
enrichment.seo_title: Inventaire Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Inventaire review covering the open-source, Wikidata-federated book inventory app, its features, self-hosting, and alternatives like Goodreads.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Inventaire is a free, open-source, peer-to-peer application for cataloguing personal book collections and connecting readers so they can lend, borrow, sell or give away books within their own social network, built by a small nonprofit collective led by French developer Maxime Lathuiliere, who began working on the project around 2015 and later formalized it, together with a co-founder known as Jums, as a French nonprofit association called Inventaire in 2019 so the founders could work on it as paid staff funded through grants, donations and consulting rather than venture capital. The project's flagship instance runs at inventaire.io, but the codebase itself is fully open source, licensed under the AGPLv3 and hosted on Codeberg, and anyone can self-host their own federated instance rather than being locked into the main site; funding has come substantially from NLnet Foundation grants supporting decentralization and self-hosting work under NGI Zero programs. Technically, Inventaire's core innovation is that its book catalog is not a proprietary database but a CC0-licensed, Wikidata-federated bibliographic graph: rather than maintaining its own siloed metadata, Inventaire reuses, extends and contributes back to Wikidata, linking books, authors, works and editions to Wikipedia, national library catalogs, and other resources in the broader Wikimedia and Linked Open Data ecosystem, so building an inventory on Inventaire also enriches an open multilingual knowledge graph anyone can query. Core features include scanning ISBN barcodes or searching titles to add books to a personal shelf, tagging each copy as available to lend, sell, or give away, browsing what people in your network or groups currently have listed, private messaging to arrange exchanges, wishlists, and multilingual support drawing on Wikidata's language coverage; there is no built-in payment or shipping system, since Inventaire is designed to facilitate local, trust-based, peer-to-peer exchanges rather than function as a commercial marketplace. Because it is entirely free, open-source software with no subscription tiers, there is no pricing structure to speak of: the flagship inventaire.io instance is free for anyone to use, and the software itself is free to download, modify and self-host, with the project sustained through grants and donations rather than user fees. The target audience is book lovers, reading groups, cooperative and community libraries, and members of the free-culture, open-data and Wikimedia communities who want a decentralized, privacy-respecting alternative to closed platforms like Goodreads or LibraryThing for tracking their book collections and sharing them socially; its direct points of comparison are Goodreads, owned by Amazon and focused on reviews and ratings rather than physical lending, LibraryThing, and open-source library-cataloguing tools, with Inventaire's clear differentiator being its federation with Wikidata, its AGPL open-source licensing, its non-commercial, community-governed nonprofit structure, and its explicit focus on facilitating real-world lending and gifting between people who know each other rather than algorithmic book discovery.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Inventaire is a free, open-source application for cataloguing personal book collections and connecting readers so they can lend, borrow, sell or give away books within their own social network. The project began around 2015 under French developer Maxime Lathuiliere and was formalized in 2019 as a French nonprofit association so its founders could work on it as paid staff.

Unlike commercial book-tracking apps, Inventaire is a community-governed, grant-funded project (notably supported by NLnet Foundation grants) rather than a venture-backed company, and its codebase is fully open source under the AGPLv3 license, with anyone free to run their own self-hosted, federated instance.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Inventaire's core innovation is that its book data is not a proprietary database but a CC0-licensed, Wikidata-federated bibliographic graph, linking books, authors and editions to Wikipedia and national library catalogs rather than maintaining a walled-garden metadata set. Users add books by scanning ISBN barcodes or searching titles, then tag copies as available to lend, sell or give away.

The platform supports private groups and networks so users can see what people they know currently have listed, private messaging to arrange exchanges, and wishlists, all with multilingual coverage inherited from Wikidata. There is deliberately no built-in payment or shipping system, since Inventaire is built around local, trust-based exchanges rather than a commercial marketplace model.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Inventaire is entirely free to use on its flagship instance at inventaire.io, with no subscription tiers, transaction fees or premium features gated behind a paywall. The project is sustained through grants (including NLnet Foundation funding) and donations rather than user fees.

Because the software is open source under AGPLv3, organizations and communities can also self-host their own federated Inventaire instance at no licensing cost, taking on only their own infrastructure costs in exchange for full control over their book data.

features.title: ISBN barcode scanning
features.description: Add books to a personal shelf by scanning barcodes or searching titles and authors.

features.title: Wikidata-federated bibliographic database
features.description: Book, author and edition data is linked to and enriches the open Wikidata knowledge graph.

features.title: Lending, selling and gifting tags
features.description: Mark individual book copies as available to lend, sell or give away to your network.

features.title: Private groups and networks
features.description: Organize contacts into groups to control who can see and request items from your inventory.

features.title: Wishlists
features.description: Track books you want so friends and network members can offer to lend or give them.

features.title: Self-hostable federated instances
features.description: Run your own independent Inventaire instance while still connecting to the shared bibliographic database.

features.title: Multilingual metadata
features.description: Book data inherits Wikidata's broad multilingual coverage for titles, authors and descriptions.

features.title: In-app messaging
features.description: Private messaging tools to coordinate lending, borrowing or gifting arrangements.

pros: Completely free with no subscription or transaction fees
Open-source (AGPLv3) and self-hostable for privacy-conscious communities
Book data is federated with Wikidata rather than siloed, improving accuracy and openness
Nonprofit, community-governed model with no advertising-driven incentives
Well suited to local lending circles, reading groups and cooperative libraries
cons: Smaller user base than mainstream platforms like Goodreads limits network effects
No integrated payments or shipping, so exchanges rely on manual, local coordination
Interface and feature set are more minimal than commercial book-tracking apps
Self-hosting a federated instance requires technical setup knowledge
Development pace is limited by a small volunteer and nonprofit team

faqs.question: What is Inventaire used for?
faqs.answer: Inventaire is used to catalog personal book collections and let your network know which books are available to lend, sell or give away.

faqs.question: Is Inventaire free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Inventaire is entirely free to use, sustained through grants and donations rather than user fees.

faqs.question: Is Inventaire open source?
faqs.answer: Yes, the codebase is open source under the AGPLv3 license and available on Codeberg.

faqs.question: How does Inventaire get its book data?
faqs.answer: Inventaire reuses and contributes to Wikidata's open bibliographic graph rather than maintaining a proprietary book database.

faqs.question: Can I self-host Inventaire?
faqs.answer: Yes, anyone can deploy their own federated Inventaire instance instead of using the main inventaire.io site.

faqs.question: What are Inventaire alternatives?
faqs.answer: Common alternatives include Goodreads, LibraryThing, and other open-source library cataloguing tools.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source, self-hosted inventory management system for parts tracking, stock control and manufacturing builds.
enrichment.seo_title: Inventree Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Inventree review covering the open-source inventory and parts management system, its BOM and build tools, plugin system and alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: InvenTree is a free, open-source inventory management and parts-tracking system created in March 2017 by Australian electrical engineer Oliver Walters, who built the original tool to manage stock and bills of materials for his own electronics and hardware projects before releasing it publicly on GitHub, where it has since grown into a project with more than 40 contributors, thousands of GitHub stars and forks, and tens of thousands of commits as of 2026. The project has no formal headquarters or venture funding; it is a community-driven open-source effort maintained by Walters and a distributed group of volunteer contributors, sustained partly through community donations and the free labor of maintainers rather than a commercial parent company, though a growing ecosystem of consultancies and hosting providers offer paid setup, customization and managed-hosting services layered on top of the free core software. Architecturally, InvenTree is built on a Python/Django backend with a PostgreSQL, MySQL or SQLite database, exposing both a web-based admin interface and a full REST API for integration with external tools, EDA software such as KiCad, and custom scripts; the frontend is a React/TypeScript single-page application, and the whole stack can be deployed via Docker, bare-metal installation, or platforms like Railway, making it accessible to hobbyists and enterprises alike. Core functionality centers on structured part categorization with custom fields and parameters, barcode and QR-code scanning for stock operations, multi-location and multi-warehouse stock tracking, intelligent Bill of Materials management that shows which sub-parts and quantities are required to build a new part, a build-order system that consumes stock to produce finished parts with automatic or manual stock allocation, purchase-order and sales-order management tied to suppliers and customers, customizable low-stock alerts, and an extensible plugin system that lets developers add custom logic, reports, labels and third-party integrations such as DigiKey, Mouser and LCSC part-data lookups without modifying core code. Because InvenTree is released under the permissive MIT license, there is no subscription fee for the core software: anyone can download, self-host, and use it for unlimited parts, stock items, users and warehouses at zero licensing cost, with the only recurring cost being the user's own server or cloud hosting bill; native and web apps exist for Android and iOS to support mobile stock-taking and barcode scanning in warehouses. The target audience is electronics manufacturers, hardware startups, makerspaces, small-to-medium manufacturers and hobbyist engineers who need structured, developer-friendly part and stock tracking without the licensing costs or vendor lock-in of commercial MRP and inventory tools; InvenTree's most direct competitors include commercial or freemium inventory platforms such as Fishbowl, Sortly, Odoo Inventory, PartKeepr and Zoho Inventory, and it differentiates itself from them chiefly through being fully open source and self-hostable, its deep BOM and build-order functionality tailored to hardware and electronics manufacturing, and its plugin-driven extensibility that lets technical teams tailor the system precisely to their workflow rather than working within a closed commercial product.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: InvenTree is a free, open-source inventory management system created in March 2017 by Australian electrical engineer Oliver Walters, originally to manage stock and bills of materials for his own electronics projects. It has since grown into a widely used community project on GitHub with dozens of contributors and a large, active user base.

Unlike a commercial vendor, InvenTree has no formal company or venture backing; it is maintained by Walters and a distributed group of volunteer contributors, sustained by community donations, with an ecosystem of third-party consultancies and hosting providers offering paid setup and managed-hosting on top of the free core software.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: InvenTree is built on a Python/Django backend with a full REST API, supporting PostgreSQL, MySQL or SQLite databases and a React/TypeScript frontend. Core functionality includes structured part categorization, barcode and QR-code scanning, multi-warehouse stock tracking, and intelligent Bill of Materials management showing exactly which sub-parts are required to build a new item.

A build-order system consumes stock to produce finished parts with automatic or manual allocation, while purchase-order and sales-order management ties suppliers and customers into the same system. An extensible plugin architecture lets developers add custom logic, labels, reports and third-party part-data integrations such as DigiKey, Mouser and LCSC without modifying the core codebase.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: InvenTree is released under the permissive MIT license, meaning the core software has no subscription fee or feature paywall: anyone can download, self-host and use it for unlimited parts, stock items, users and warehouses at zero licensing cost.

The only recurring cost is the organization's own server or cloud hosting bill. For teams without in-house DevOps resources, third-party consultancies and hosting providers offer paid deployment, customization and managed-hosting services on top of the free software.

features.description: Organize parts into structured categories with custom fields and parameters.

features.description: Scan barcodes or QR codes to speed up stock check-in, check-out and audits.

features.description: Define BOMs showing which sub-parts and quantities are needed to build a new part.

features.title: Build order tracking

features.description: Track orders tied to suppliers and customers alongside stock levels.

features.title: REST API and plugin system
features.description: Full API access and an extensible plugin architecture for custom integrations.

features.title: Multi-warehouse stock locations
features.description: Track stock across multiple physical locations and warehouses.

features.title: Mobile apps
features.description: Native Android and iOS apps for on-the-floor stock-taking and barcode scanning.

pros: Completely free and open source under the permissive MIT license
Deep BOM and build-order tools tailored to electronics and hardware manufacturing
Self-hosted, so data stays fully under the organization's control
Extensible via a plugin architecture and full REST API
Active, fast-moving open-source community with frequent releases
cons: Requires technical know-how (Docker, Python, database administration) to deploy and maintain
No official vendor support; help comes from community channels or paid consultancies
UI/UX is more utilitarian than polished commercial inventory tools
Self-hosting means the user bears responsibility for backups, security and uptime
Advanced features like plugins may require developer skills to configure

faqs.question: What is Inventree used for?
faqs.answer: Inventree is an open-source system for tracking parts, stock levels, bills of materials and manufacturing builds.

faqs.question: Is Inventree free?
faqs.answer: Yes, the core software is free and open source under the MIT license, with no seat fees or feature paywalls.

faqs.question: Does Inventree require self-hosting?
faqs.answer: Yes, Inventree is designed to be self-hosted, though third-party providers offer paid managed-hosting options.

faqs.question: Can Inventree manage bills of materials and builds?
faqs.answer: Yes, it includes intelligent BOM management and a build-order system that consumes stock to produce finished parts.

faqs.question: Is there a mobile app for Inventree?
faqs.answer: Yes, native Android and iOS apps support barcode scanning and stock-taking on the go.

faqs.question: What are Inventree alternatives?
faqs.answer: Common alternatives include Fishbowl, Sortly, Odoo Inventory, PartKeepr and Zoho Inventory.

enrichment.short_description: Cloud-based ITSM and IT service desk platform with AI-assisted ticketing, low-code workflows and ITIL processes.
enrichment.pricing_model: Subscription (per-agent tiered) plus Enterprise custom pricing
enrichment.seo_title: InvGate Service Desk Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: InvGate Service Desk review covering ITIL features, transparent per-agent pricing, AI Service Agent, and comparisons to Freshservice and Jira Service Management.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: InvGate is an IT service management (ITSM) software company founded in 2008 in Buenos Aires, Argentina, by Ariel Gesto, who serves as CEO, and Liliana Gary, who serves as President, built to offer enterprise-grade IT and service management tools without the heavy implementation costs and lengthy consulting engagements typical of legacy ITSM vendors. InvGate has grown into a global company with roughly 250 to 265 employees spread across five continents and reports around 30 million dollars in annual revenue, remaining privately held and Latin-American-founded while serving customers such as universities, government agencies and mid-size to large enterprises worldwide; the flagship product line spans InvGate Service Desk alongside a broader InvGate Service Management and asset management suite. Architecturally, InvGate Service Desk is a cloud-hosted SaaS ITSM platform with an on-premise option available at the Enterprise tier, built around a low-code workflow engine that lets administrators configure ticket types, approval chains, SLAs and OLAs and custom fields without needing professional services engagements, which the company markets as enabling go-live in a matter of weeks rather than months. Core features include ITIL-aligned incident, problem and change management, a self-service portal and knowledge base, a drag-and-drop workflow and automation builder, an AI-powered virtual service agent that can triage and resolve common requests, SLA and OLA tracking with escalation rules, multi-department and multi-brand support desks, asset and CMDB integration when paired with InvGate's asset management product, native integrations with Microsoft Teams, Slack, WhatsApp, Active Directory and SSO providers, and built-in reporting and analytics dashboards. Pricing is tiered and published transparently on InvGate's website, unlike many ITSM competitors that hide pricing behind a sales call: the Starter plan is a fixed 1,499 dollars per year for up to 5 agents with unlimited end users, SSO and basic AI hub features; the Pro plan starts around 500 dollars per agent per year, from roughly 2,500 dollars annually, for 5 to 50 agents and adds the visual workflow builder, the AI Service Agent, and Teams and WhatsApp integrations; and the Enterprise plan starts around 12,000 dollars per year with custom or unlimited agent pricing, adding on-premise deployment, concurrent seat licensing, multiple SAML and SSO configurations and dedicated support; all tiers include unlimited end users and requesters at no extra charge, which differentiates InvGate's pricing model from per-seat helpdesk tools that charge for every user who submits a ticket. The target audience is small-to-mid-size IT teams as well as larger enterprises, including higher education and government, that want ITIL-aligned service management without ServiceNow-level cost and complexity; InvGate's direct competitors include Freshservice, Jira Service Management, Zendesk for IT, SysAid and ServiceNow, and it differentiates on transparent, comparatively affordable per-agent pricing, unlimited free end-users, fast low-code implementation, and Latin-American-headquartered support and pricing that tends to undercut US and European ITSM incumbents.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: InvGate is an IT service management company founded in 2008 in Buenos Aires, Argentina, by CEO Ariel Gesto and President Liliana Gary, built to deliver ITIL-aligned service desk software without the heavy consulting costs of legacy ITSM vendors. The company has grown into a global operation with employees across five continents while remaining privately held.

InvGate Service Desk is the company's flagship ITSM product, part of a broader InvGate Service Management suite that also includes IT asset management. It is positioned as a fast-to-implement, low-code alternative to enterprise incumbents like ServiceNow.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The platform provides ITIL-aligned incident, problem and change management, a self-service portal and knowledge base, and a low-code drag-and-drop workflow builder that lets administrators configure ticket types, approval chains and SLAs without professional services. An AI-powered virtual service agent triages and resolves common requests automatically.

InvGate supports multi-department and multi-brand support desks, SLA and OLA tracking with escalation rules, and native integrations with Microsoft Teams, Slack, WhatsApp and SSO providers, along with reporting dashboards and optional CMDB integration through InvGate's asset management product.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: InvGate publishes its pricing transparently: Starter costs a fixed 1,499 dollars per year for up to 5 agents, Pro starts around 500 dollars per agent per year for 5 to 50 agents and adds the workflow builder and AI Service Agent, and Enterprise starts around 12,000 dollars per year with custom, unlimited agent pricing and on-premise deployment options.

A distinguishing feature of InvGate's pricing model is that end users and approvers are unlimited and free on every tier, so organizations only pay for agent seats rather than every employee who submits a ticket, a structure that tends to undercut per-seat competitors for organizations with large end-user bases.

features.title: ITIL-aligned incident and change management

features.title: Self-service portal and knowledge base

features.description: Drag-and-drop configuration of ticket types, approval chains and automation rules.

features.title: AI Service Agent
features.description: AI-powered virtual agent that triages and resolves common IT requests automatically.

features.title: SLA and OLA management
features.description: Service-level tracking with escalation rules to keep tickets moving within agreed timeframes.

features.title: Multi-department and multi-brand desks
features.description: Support multiple departments or brands from a single InvGate instance.

features.title: Microsoft Teams, Slack and WhatsApp integrations
features.description: Native integrations letting end users and agents interact through familiar communication channels.

features.title: Reporting and analytics
features.description: Built-in dashboards for tracking ticket volume, SLA compliance and team performance.

pros: Transparent published pricing, unusual among enterprise ITSM vendors
Unlimited free end-users and requesters on every plan
Fast, low-code implementation measured in weeks rather than months
ITIL-aligned processes without ServiceNow-level cost
AI Service Agent helps automate first-line triage
cons: Starter plan is limited to 5 agents, requiring an upgrade for growing teams
Advanced integrations like Teams, WhatsApp and the AI agent are gated behind Pro/Enterprise tiers
On-premise deployment is only available at the highest-cost Enterprise tier
Smaller third-party integration ecosystem than ServiceNow or Jira Service Management
Some reviewers note reporting customization is less flexible than larger competitors

faqs.question: What is InvGate Service Desk used for?
faqs.answer: InvGate Service Desk is an ITSM platform used to manage IT tickets, incidents, changes and requests following ITIL best practices.

faqs.question: How much does InvGate Service Desk cost?
faqs.answer: Pricing starts at 1,499 dollars per year for the Starter plan (5 agents), with Pro from roughly 500 dollars per agent per year and Enterprise from around 12,000 dollars per year.

faqs.question: Is InvGate Service Desk ITIL-compliant?
faqs.answer: Yes, it supports ITIL-aligned incident, problem, change and request management processes.

faqs.question: Does InvGate offer on-premise deployment?
faqs.answer: Yes, on-premise deployment is available at the Enterprise tier, while lower tiers are cloud-hosted SaaS.

faqs.question: Does InvGate include AI features?
faqs.answer: Yes, an AI Service Agent is available on Pro and Enterprise plans to triage and resolve common requests automatically.

faqs.question: What are InvGate Service Desk alternatives?
faqs.answer: Common alternatives include Freshservice, Jira Service Management, Zendesk for IT, SysAid and ServiceNow.

enrichment.short_description: AI-powered online video creation platform that turns text prompts, scripts or blog posts into edited marketing videos.
enrichment.seo_title: InVideo Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: InVideo review covering AI text-to-video generation, Sora 2 and Veo 3.1 access, pricing tiers, features, and alternatives like Synthesia and Pictory.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: InVideo is an AI-powered online video creation platform founded in 2017 by Sanket Shah, who serves as CEO, along with Harsh Vakharia, Pankit Chheda and Anshul Khandelwal, originally built as a template-based video editor for marketers before pivoting into generative AI video creation; the company is headquartered in San Francisco, California, with a large engineering and operations team based in Mumbai, India, reflecting its roots as an India-founded startup that later re-incorporated its holding structure in the US as it scaled internationally. InVideo has raised approximately 52.5 million dollars in total funding, including a Series B round backed by investors such as Peak XV Partners (formerly Sequoia Capital India) and Tiger Global, and by 2026 the company reports roughly 70 million dollars in annual recurring revenue with tens of millions of cumulative users across more than 150 countries, figures the company has cited as making it one of the most capital-efficient AI SaaS companies to emerge from India. Its flagship product, InVideo AI, lets users generate a complete edited video from a single text prompt, script, blog-post URL or product link: the AI writes a script, selects stock footage, images and music, generates voiceovers including cloned voices, adds captions and subtitles, and assembles a rough cut that users can then refine in a full timeline-based editor; the platform also offers AI avatars and talking presenters, automatic translation and dubbing into multiple languages, a large library of templates, and access to premium stock libraries such as iStock. A notable technical differentiator as of 2026 is that InVideo bundles access to multiple third-party generative video and voice models, including OpenAI's Sora 2 and Google's Veo 3.1, inside a single subscription, letting users switch between underlying AI video engines without separate accounts or billing relationships. Pricing follows a freemium SaaS model: a free tier lets users try AI video generation with watermarked, limited exports; the Plus plan runs roughly 20 dollars per month billed annually, or about 25 to 28 dollars billed monthly, and includes around 50 AI generation minutes per month, a set number of iStock downloads, a couple of cloned voices and roughly 120 minutes of voiceover; the Max plan runs roughly 48 dollars per month billed annually, or about 60 dollars billed monthly, and roughly doubles those AI generation minutes, iStock downloads and voice clones; and higher business plans extend up toward several hundred dollars per month for teams needing higher AI-minute quotas, more seats and premium support. The target audience spans solo content creators, social media marketers, small business owners, and marketing teams at larger companies who need to produce short-form marketing, explainer, ad and social videos quickly without traditional video-editing skills; InVideo's most direct competitors include Synthesia for enterprise AI avatar video, Pictory for content repurposing and summarization, HeyGen for AI avatar video, Veed.io for timeline editing plus AI tools, Runway for generative video and creative experimentation, and Canva's video tools, and InVideo differentiates itself primarily through its prompt-to-finished-video workflow aimed specifically at fast social and marketing content, its bundling of multiple frontier AI video models in one subscription, and pricing that undercuts pure-enterprise tools like Synthesia while offering more automation than manual timeline editors like Veed.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: InVideo is an AI-powered video creation platform founded in 2017 by Sanket Shah, Harsh Vakharia, Pankit Chheda and Anshul Khandelwal, originally launched as a template-based editor for marketers before pivoting into generative AI video. The company is headquartered in San Francisco with a large team based in Mumbai, India.

InVideo has raised roughly 52.5 million dollars in funding from investors including Peak XV Partners and Tiger Global, and reports around 70 million dollars in annual recurring revenue as of 2026 with tens of millions of users across 150-plus countries.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: InVideo AI turns a single text prompt, script, blog post or product link into a complete edited video: it writes the script, selects stock footage and music, generates voiceovers with optional voice cloning, adds captions, and assembles a rough cut ready for review in a full timeline-based editor.

The platform also offers AI avatars, automatic translation and dubbing, and a large template library, and as of 2026 it bundles access to multiple third-party generative video models, including OpenAI's Sora 2 and Google's Veo 3.1, inside a single subscription rather than requiring separate accounts for each model.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: InVideo offers a free tier for testing AI video generation with watermarked, limited exports. The Plus plan runs roughly 20 dollars per month billed annually (25-28 dollars billed monthly) with around 50 AI generation minutes, while the Max plan runs roughly 48 dollars per month billed annually (about 60 dollars monthly) with roughly double the AI generation minutes and voice clones.

Higher business and enterprise-style plans extend pricing up toward several hundred dollars per month for teams that need larger AI-minute quotas, more seats and premium support; because generation minutes are metered, actual monthly cost can vary significantly with usage, especially when using premium models like Sora 2.

features.description: Generate a full edited video from a text prompt, script, blog post URL or product link.

features.description: AI-generated talking presenters and cloned voices for narration and presentation-style videos.

features.description: AI writes a video script and selects matching stock footage, images and music automatically.

features.title: Multi-language translation and dubbing
features.description: Translate and dub videos into multiple languages for international audiences.

features.title: Timeline-based manual editor
features.description: Full editing timeline to refine AI-generated rough cuts with manual control.

features.title: Access to Sora 2 and Veo 3.1
features.description: Bundled access to OpenAI's Sora 2 and Google's Veo 3.1 generative video models in one subscription.

features.title: Stock media library
features.description: Integrated iStock footage, image and music library for video assembly.

features.title: Marketing and social templates
features.description: Large library of templates tailored to social media and marketing video formats.

pros: Fast prompt-to-video workflow requires no editing experience
Bundles multiple frontier AI video models, including Sora 2 and Veo 3.1, in one subscription
Free tier available to test AI generation before paying
Strong template library aimed at marketing and social content
Voice cloning and multilingual dubbing built in
cons: AI generation minutes are capped per plan and can run out quickly for heavy users
Higher-quality outputs from premium models often consume credits faster
Free plan exports carry watermarks and limited resolution
Manual editor is less full-featured than dedicated tools like Premiere or Veed for complex edits
Pricing can climb quickly for teams needing high-volume AI generation or multiple seats

faqs.question: What is InVideo used for?
faqs.answer: InVideo is used to generate and edit marketing, social media and explainer videos from text prompts, scripts or existing content.

faqs.question: Is InVideo free?
faqs.answer: InVideo offers a free tier with watermarked, limited exports; paid plans start around 20 dollars per month billed annually.

faqs.question: Does InVideo support AI avatars and voice cloning?
faqs.answer: Yes, InVideo offers AI avatars and voice cloning as part of its generation tools.

faqs.question: What AI video models does InVideo use?
faqs.answer: InVideo bundles access to models including OpenAI's Sora 2 and Google's Veo 3.1 alongside its own generation engine.

faqs.question: Can I edit AI-generated videos manually in InVideo?
faqs.answer: Yes, a full timeline-based editor lets users manually refine any AI-generated rough cut.

faqs.question: What are InVideo alternatives?
faqs.answer: Common alternatives include Synthesia, Pictory, HeyGen, Veed.io, Runway and Canva's video tools.

use_cases.description: Small businesses create product and explainer videos without a dedicated editing team.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source invoicing, billing and payments platform for freelancers and small businesses, self-hosted or cloud-hosted.
enrichment.starting_price: Free (self-hosted or up to 5 clients); hosted Pro from $14/month
enrichment.seo_title: Invoice Ninja Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Invoice Ninja review covering the open-source, self-hostable invoicing platform, its pricing tiers, features, and alternatives like FreshBooks and Zoho.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Invoice Ninja is an invoicing, quoting, payments and time-tracking platform co-founded in 2014 by Hillel Coren and Shalom Stark, who met over coffee in late 2013 to plan the product and had it live by March 2014; developer Dave Michael joined as CTO in 2015, and the company has remained a small, entirely bootstrapped team based in Haifa, Israel, with no outside venture funding, growing the platform to over 200,000 accounts by combining a free, permissively-licensed self-hosted product with a paid hosted SaaS version. The software itself is built with a Laravel PHP backend and Flutter- and React-based frontends and mobile apps, and version 5, the current major version as of 2026, was a ground-up rewrite of the original version 4 codebase; source code is publicly available on GitHub, but since the version 5 rewrite the project has been licensed under the Elastic License v2 rather than a traditional OSI open-source license, meaning anyone can view, self-host and modify the code for their own business use, but reselling it or offering it as a hosted service to third parties requires a separate commercial agreement with the company. Core functionality includes creating and customizing invoices, quotes, purchase orders and credit notes with multiple templates, recurring and subscription billing with automated payment reminders, a client portal where customers can view and pay invoices online, online payment processing through more than 40 gateways including Stripe, PayPal, Square and Braintree, expense and time tracking tied to projects and tasks, basic inventory tracking for product-based businesses, multi-currency and multi-language support, e-invoicing and PEPPOL network connectivity for European tax compliance, and a full REST API plus native desktop apps for Windows, macOS and Linux and mobile apps for iOS and Android alongside the web app. Pricing has two tracks: self-hosting is completely free forever with no artificial limits on clients, invoices or features, the cost is simply the user's own server, while the hosted cloud version offers a free plan capped at 5 clients, a Pro plan around 14 dollars per month, or roughly 140 dollars per year with two months free on annual billing, that unlocks unlimited clients, custom branding, a branded URL, full template customization and API access, an Enterprise plan priced per number of users, from around 18 dollars per month for 1-2 users scaling up to roughly 300 dollars per month for 51-100 users, that adds multi-user permissions, file attachments, bank feed sync and bundled e-invoicing and PEPPOL credits, and a custom-priced Premium Business tier from roughly 280 dollars per year that adds a developer concierge, priority support and white-glove data migration. The target audience is freelancers, consultants, agencies and small businesses that want either a zero-cost, self-hosted, data-sovereign billing system or an affordable hosted alternative to expensive SaaS incumbents; Invoice Ninja's most direct competitors are FreshBooks, QuickBooks, Zoho Invoice, Wave and other open-source options like Crater, and it differentiates itself chiefly through its self-hosting option, rare among modern invoicing SaaS products, its source-available codebase, generous free tier, and bootstrapped, freelancer-friendly pricing well below FreshBooks or QuickBooks equivalents.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Invoice Ninja is an invoicing, quoting, payments and time-tracking platform co-founded in 2014 by Hillel Coren and Shalom Stark, with Dave Michael joining as CTO in 2015. The company is a small, entirely bootstrapped team based in Haifa, Israel, with no outside venture funding, having grown the platform to over 200,000 accounts.

The current major version, v5, is a ground-up rewrite of the original v4 codebase, built on a Laravel PHP backend with Flutter and React-based frontends and mobile apps. Source code is public on GitHub under the Elastic License v2, which allows self-hosting and modification but restricts reselling the software as a hosted service to third parties.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Invoice Ninja supports creating and customizing invoices, quotes, purchase orders and credit notes across multiple templates, recurring and subscription billing with automated payment reminders, and a client portal where customers can view and pay invoices online through more than 40 payment gateways including Stripe, PayPal, Square and Braintree.

The platform also includes expense and time tracking tied to projects and tasks, basic inventory tracking, multi-currency and multi-language support, e-invoicing and PEPPOL network connectivity for European tax compliance, and a full REST API alongside native desktop apps for Windows, macOS and Linux and mobile apps for iOS and Android.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Self-hosting Invoice Ninja is completely free forever with no artificial limits on clients, invoices or features, the only cost being the user's own server. On the hosted cloud side, a free plan is capped at 5 clients, while the Pro plan costs around 14 dollars per month and unlocks unlimited clients, custom branding and API access.

The Enterprise plan is priced per number of users, from around 18 dollars per month for 1-2 users up to roughly 300 dollars per month for 51-100 users, and adds multi-user permissions, bank feed sync and bundled e-invoicing/PEPPOL credits, while a custom-priced Premium Business tier adds a developer concierge and white-glove data migration for larger teams.

features.title: Customizable invoices and quotes
features.description: Create branded invoices, quotes, purchase orders and credit notes across multiple templates.

features.title: Recurring billing and reminders
features.description: Automate subscription billing cycles and send automated payment reminders to clients.

features.title: Client portal with online payments
features.description: Clients view and pay invoices online through more than 40 supported payment gateways.

features.title: Time and expense tracking
features.description: Track billable time and expenses tied to specific projects and tasks.

features.title: E-invoicing and PEPPOL compliance
features.description: Support for e-invoicing standards and the PEPPOL network for European tax compliance.

features.title: Full REST API
features.description: Programmatic access for integrating invoicing and billing data with other business systems.

features.title: Multi-currency and multi-language support
features.description: Bill clients in their local currency and language across international markets.

features.title: Self-hosting option
features.description: Deploy the full platform on your own server with no artificial client or feature limits.

pros: Free, unlimited self-hosted version with no artificial feature caps
Affordable hosted plans compared to FreshBooks or QuickBooks
Supports 40-plus payment gateways and multi-currency invoicing
Native desktop and mobile apps alongside the web app
Bootstrapped company with a long track record and stable pricing
cons: Self-hosting requires technical setup with a PHP/Laravel server and database maintenance
The Elastic License v2 restricts reselling or offering it as a hosted service to others
Interface feels less polished than some modern SaaS competitors
Free hosted plan is capped at just 5 clients
Advanced features like bank sync and e-invoicing credits are reserved for higher tiers

faqs.question: Is Invoice Ninja free?
faqs.answer: Yes, self-hosting is free forever with no client or invoice limits, and the hosted version includes a free plan capped at 5 clients.

faqs.question: Can I self-host Invoice Ninja?
faqs.answer: Yes, Invoice Ninja can be self-hosted for free via manual install, Docker, or a one-click auto-installer.

faqs.question: What payment gateways does Invoice Ninja support?
faqs.answer: Invoice Ninja supports more than 40 payment gateways, including Stripe, PayPal, Square and Braintree.

faqs.question: Is Invoice Ninja open source?
faqs.answer: The source code is publicly available under the Elastic License v2, which allows self-hosting and modification but restricts commercial resale.

faqs.question: Does Invoice Ninja support recurring invoices?
faqs.answer: Yes, it supports recurring and subscription billing with automated payment reminders.

faqs.question: What are Invoice Ninja alternatives?
faqs.answer: Common alternatives include FreshBooks, QuickBooks, Zoho Invoice, Wave and the open-source tool Crater.

use_cases.title: Freelance and consultant billing
use_cases.description: Freelancers and consultants bill clients directly with recurring invoices and online payments.

use_cases.title: Agency time and billing management
use_cases.description: Small agencies track billable time and expenses across client projects and invoice accordingly.

use_cases.title: Self-hosted, privacy-focused billing
use_cases.description: Privacy-conscious businesses self-host their billing data instead of using a third-party SaaS provider.
